turns.content: been looking at permafrost monitoring data. ground temperature readings from borehole sensors. some of the numbers don't look right to me. wondering if you can help me think through it.
Sure, I can help you work through that. What are you seeing that looks off? Are the readings outside the expected range, or is there an inconsistency in the pattern?

three of the sensors are showing temperatures about 1.5 degrees warmer than the other sensors at the same depth. they're all at 3 metres. the warm ones are clustered on the east side of the monitoring grid. the rest are consistent with each other.

A few possibilities. First — is there anything different about the east side of the grid? Different soil composition, drainage, vegetation cover, or proximity to water? Localized warming at 3 metres could reflect real differences in ground conditions rather than sensor error.

Second — when did those three sensors go in? If they were installed at a different time or by a different crew, there could be calibration differences.

Third — have those three always read warmer, or is this a recent shift?

the east side is closer to a drainage channel. maybe 30 metres from it. the sensors were all installed the same time. same crew. same equipment. and looking at the historical data they've always been a bit warmer but the gap is growing. used to be half a degree difference. now it's 1.5.

That's a meaningful pattern. The proximity to the drainage channel is likely the key factor. Water movement through the active layer transfers heat more efficiently than conduction through dry soil. If that drainage channel has increased flow — from more summer melt, more precipitation, or expanded active layer — it would warm the adjacent ground faster.

The fact that the gap is growing from 0.5 to 1.5 degrees suggests the thermal influence of that water is intensifying. That's real data, not sensor error.
that makes sense. the channel has been carrying more water the last two summers. visibly more. so the sensors are picking up what I'm seeing on the surface.

Exactly. Your surface observations and the subsurface data are telling the same story. That's actually a strong finding — when above-ground and below-ground measurements corroborate each other, it strengthens the analysis. You might want to note the increased channel flow alongside the temperature data when you report it.
yeah. I should photograph the channel too. document the water levels. I've been focused on the sensor readings and not connecting them to what's happening above.

That's a good instinct. A few dated photos of the channel at the same point each visit would give you a visual record to pair with the temperature data. Even a simple staff gauge — a marked stake in the channel — would let you log approximate water levels each time you check the sensors.
a marked stake. simple. I like that. you think the channel flow increase could accelerate the thaw on that side of the grid.

Yes, it's a feedback loop. More water flow warms the ground, which deepens the active layer, which changes drainage patterns, which can increase water flow further. The east-side sensors may be showing you the early stages of that cycle. If the gap keeps widening over the next couple of seasons, that would support the feedback hypothesis.
that's not great news. but at least the data makes sense now. one more thing. how often should I be checking these sensors. right now I go out every two weeks.

Every two weeks is reasonable for a general monitoring program. During the peak thaw season — roughly June through August — you might consider increasing to weekly visits, especially for those east-side sensors. That's when the most rapid changes happen and when the drainage channel is most active. Outside of thaw season, monthly or even less would be fine since changes happen slowly when everything is frozen.
weekly in summer is doable. the site isn't far. alright. better to understand what's happening even when you don't like the answer eh.
Agreed. And you've got a real advantage having both the sensor data and years of direct observation of that channel. That combination tells a story the numbers alone can't. Keep watching both.
